translatedText: In 1902, he took the world's first aerial photographs of Antarctica from a hot air balloon at an altitude of 240m.
Discovery of the Overland Route to the South Pole
In 1902, he became the first to discover the overland route leading to the South Pole.
When and where was Ernest Shackleton born?
Ernest Shackleton was born on February 15, 1874, in Ireland.
Did Ernest Shackleton reach the South Pole?
In 1908, he fell short of the South Pole by 155km due to a lack of food, but in 1909 he reached a record furthest south latitude of 88° 23'.
What is Ernest Shackleton's most famous achievement?
During the Endurance expedition in 1916, he managed to rescue all 27 of his crew members without a single loss of life amidst the extreme conditions of Antarctica.
How did Ernest Shackleton die?
He died of a fatal heart attack caused by atheroma on January 5, 1922, while his ship, the Quest, was moored at Stromness Bay in South Georgia.
What honors did Ernest Shackleton receive?

He was appointed a Member of the Royal Victorian Order (4th class) in 1907, a Commander of the Royal Victorian Order (3rd class) and knighted in 1909, and awarded an Officer of the Order of the British Empire (Military Division) in 1919.
Birth of Franz Peter Schubert
Began receiving musical instruction from his father at age 5
Enrolled in his father's school and began formal music education
Took lessons from Michael Holzer at age 7; received instruction from Antonio Salieri
Admitted as a scholarship student to the Stadtkonvikt (Imperial Seminary)
Left the Stadtkonvikt at age 13 due to his voice breaking and poor academic performance
Began working as a teaching assistant at his father's primary school; composed the Mass in F major
Composed hundreds of songs at age 18; wrote 'Erlkönig' (The Erlking), 'Rastlose Liebe' (Restless Love), and 'Heidenröslein' (Rose on the Heath)
At the urging of Franz von Schober, he moved into his friend's house and dedicated himself to composing; began a bohemian lifestyle; believed to have contracted syphilis
Met Johann Michael Vogl
Composed the songs 'Der Tod und das Mädchen' (Death and the Maiden) and 'Die Forelle' (The Trout); became a music tutor to the Esterházy family

Traveled with Vogl to perform in Upper Austria; his music was performed publicly for the first time by Vogl; failed in his attempt to secure a teaching post in Laibach; composed the song 'Der Wanderer' and Symphony No. 5
Composed Symphony No. 8 'Unfinished'
Published 'Wanderer Fantasy' and 'Rosamunde'; composed the song cycle 'Die schöne Müllerin' (The Fair Maid of the Mill)
Returned to the Esterházy family as a music teacher; composed String Quartets
Traveled with Vogl to Western Austria; composed 'Ave Maria'
Composed the song cycle 'Winterreise' (Winter Journey) at age 30; visited Beethoven at his home on March 19; attended Beethoven's funeral on March 26

Held a highly successful private concert in March; completed Symphony No. 9 'The Great'; finished most of the songs in 'Schwanengesang' (Swan Song) by August; fell ill with a mysterious illness on November 17; died prematurely in Vienna on November 19 at the age of 31
Founder of the 19th-Century German Lied Form
Regarded as the founder of the 19th-century German Lied (art song), leaving behind numerous outstanding masterpieces in this genre.
'King of Lieder'
He is known as the 'King of Lieder' (King of Songs) having composed over 600 vocal works.
Major Song Cycles
Left behind remarkable song cycles such as 'Winterreise', 'Die schöne Müllerin', and 'Schwanengesang'.
Major Symphonies
Symphony No. 8 'Unfinished' and Symphony No. 9 'The Great' are particularly famous.
Piano Quintet 'The Trout'
Composed the Piano Quintet in A major, 'The Trout', which features a unique instrumentation using a double bass instead of a second violin in the string quartet.
Lied 'Erlkönig'
Composed the famous Lied 'Erlkönig' (The Erlking), based on a poem by Goethe, at the age of 17.
Why is Schubert called the 'King of Lieder'?
Because he composed over 600 vocal works, particularly excelling in the Lieder format, and is widely regarded as the founder of the 19th-century German Lied.
Why has there been confusion regarding the numbering of Schubert's symphonies?
Unpublished works were continually discovered and reorganized after his death, leading to several changes in numbering, and different numbering systems are still used interchangeably today.
Did Schubert ever meet Beethoven?
Yes, Schubert visited Beethoven on March 19, 1827, just a week before Beethoven's death, marking their first and final meeting.
What is the presumed cause of Schubert's death?
While questions surrounding Schubert's death remain unsolved, it is now widely believed that he died from complications of syphilis.
What are Schubert's most representative song cycles?
His most famous song cycles include 'Winterreise' (Winter Journey), 'Die schöne Müllerin' (The Fair Maid of the Mill), and the posthumously published 'Schwanengesang' (Swan Song).
January 24, 1712
Birth of Frederick II
August 5, 1730
Attempted to flee the country with the help of Army Lieutenant Hans Hermann von Katte and Second Lieutenant Peter Karl Christoph von Keith
November 18, 1730
Released and pardoned by his father, the King
Married Elisabeth Christine
Ascended the throne as Frederick II after the death of Frederick William I
December 16, 1740
The Prussian army advanced into Silesia, sparking the War of the Austrian Succession
Signed the Treaty of Breslau, taking control of almost all of Silesia
Occupied and annexed East Frisia following the death of Charles Edzard, Prince of East Frisia
August 1744
Advanced an army of 80,000 into Bohemia
December 24, 1745
Signed the Treaty of Dresden, recognizing Prussia's claim to Silesia and securing an indemnity
August 29, 1756
Invaded the Electorate of Saxony, beginning the Seven Years' War
October 15, 1756
Saxony surrendered
Suffered a major defeat by the Austrian army at the Battle of Kolin
August 12, 1759
Battle of Kunersdorf
October 1760
The Austrian army besieged Berlin
January 5, 1762
The sudden death of Empress Elizabeth of Russia and the accession of Peter III led to peace between Prussia and Russia
February 10, 1763
Signed a peace treaty confirming Prussia's sovereignty over Silesia
Participated in the First Partition of Poland and annexed West Prussia
Fought against Austria in the War of the Bavarian Succession
August 17, 1786
Died at Sanssouci Palace in Potsdam
Introduced to Korea by Yu Kil-chun through his work
After German reunification, he was interred in the courtyard of Sanssouci Palace alongside his dogs, in accordance with his will
Policy of Religious Tolerance and Abolition of Torture
He promoted a policy of religious tolerance and abolished torture in judicial proceedings.
Growth into a Prussian Military Power
With his outstanding military talent and rational state management, he transformed Prussia into the strongest military power in Europe at the time.
Securing Silesia and Establishing Prussian Hegemony
He won wars against Austria, seized the territory of Silesia, and established Prussian hegemony within the Holy Roman Empire.
Implementation of National Conscription
He implemented a system of national conscription to build military strength, intensifying the military training of Prussian men.
Expansion of General Education and Drafting a Written Constitution
He pursued tolerant policies toward all religions, expanded general education, and participated in the drafting of a written constitution.
Revival of the Berlin Academy
Unlike his predecessor who despised academia, he revived the Berlin Academy and recalled scholars who had previously been exiled.
Securing West Prussia
He participated in the First Partition of Poland in 1772, annexing West Prussia, which was then Polish territory, to expand his borders.
How is Frederick II evaluated?

He is evaluated as an enlightened despot who enacted policies of religious tolerance and abolished torture, and as a leader whose exceptional military talent and rational governance transformed Prussia into the strongest military power in Europe.
What major policies did Frederick II implement?
He implemented policies of religious tolerance, the abolition of torture in trials, the expansion of general education, national conscription, and the drafting of a written constitution.
What led to Frederick II being called 'the Great'?

After his victory in the War of the Austrian Succession, he was given the title 'the Great' upon signing the Treaty of Dresden in 1745, which implied a status effectively equal to that of the Emperor.
What is the representative book written by Frederick II?
He authored 'Anti-Machiavel', a work that opposed Machiavelli's ideas of political deceit and cunning.
Did Frederick II have any children?
Although he married Elisabeth Christine, they had no children together. He was succeeded by his nephew, Frederick William II.
Birth
Princess Victoria invited her to her coronation
Death
Britain's First Female Sociologist
She is often cited as the first female sociologist in Britain and a prominent social theorist.
Translation of Auguste Comte's Work
She translated the works of Auguste Comte.
Economic Independence
